primary_diagnosis.name: Acute irritant/allergic contact dermatitis of the scalp superimposed on androgenetic alopecia

pathophysiology_summary: An unregulated concentrated topical compound applied to the scalp produced an acute irritant (and possibly allergic) contact dermatitis: erythema, oedema, micro-vesiculation and serous weeping sharply confined to the application area, beginning within an hour. Discriminators: intact epidermis with vesicles rather than eschar/coagulative necrosis argues against a full-thickness chemical burn; hyper-acute onset precisely matching product application with no fever argues against cellulitis; the acute painful vesicular weeping is far too severe/rapid for seborrhoeic dermatitis. The baseline androgenetic alopecia is an incidental distractor. Management is decontamination (already washed), potent topical corticosteroid, oral antihistamine, product cessation, and a clear airway/anaphylaxis and superinfection safety-net (any facial/throat swelling or breathing difficulty is an emergency).

differential.diagnosis: Acute contact dermatitis of the scalp
differential.evidence_basis: Hyper-acute weeping erythema exactly at the product application site.
Concentrated unknown chemical could cause a burn; assess for eschar/coagulative necrosis vs weeping vesicles.
Must screen for facial/airway swelling after a chemical exposure.
Consider if fever/spreading demarcated hot erythema or pus — absent, and timing fits contact reaction.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Screen for airway involvement first; then treat as contact dermatitis. Do not anchor on the baseline hair loss.

differential.diagnosis: Acute contact dermatitis of the scalp
differential.evidence_basis: Vesicular weeping, intact epidermis, no airway involvement, clear (not purulent) exudate.
Intact epidermis with weeping vesicles rather than eschar/white coagulation.
No facial/airway swelling or breathing difficulty on screening.

differential.diagnosis: Acute contact dermatitis of the scalp
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis: decontamination, potent topical steroid, antihistamine, product cessation, airway/infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Facial/periorbital/lip/tongue/throat swelling or any breathing or swallowing difficulty
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate evolving systemic allergy/anaphylaxis or airway angioedema requiring adrenaline and emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed evolving anaphylaxis/airway swelling can be fatal.

red_flags.description: Thick foul-smelling purulent discharge / spreading warmth (secondary infection)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Weeping broken skin can superinfect; would need antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated superinfection could progress to cellulitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating to a burns unit for an intact-epidermis contact dermatitis.
Not asking about facial/throat swelling after a chemical exposure.
Being distracted by the underlying androgenetic alopecia rather than the acute reaction.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Advise immediate copious rinsing (done), start potent topical steroid + antihistamine, discard product; ED/000 if any facial/airway swelling or breathing difficulty.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing evolving airway angioedema after a chemical exposure, or over-treating as a full-thickness burn.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute contact dermatitis: remove the irritant, apply a potent topical corticosteroid, add an oral antihistamine for symptom relief, and safety-net for infection/systemic allergy.
